verbatim_text: Now, why is it static? Because at test time, when you feed it an input, it performs a fixed set of computations or operations before it makes a prediction. Now, this paradigm of static models is not really limiting in theory. We can represent complex functions. But what we see in practice is that whenever there's a hard instance, for example, there's a shift in distribution from your training images to the test image, then no matter how big the static model was, they typically end up failing. Now, as inputs get harder, what's more clear is that we need models with variable number of operations. That is, these are models that can adapt to the test instance. So, for example, consider this image that's given to this model. It's an image classification task. So this model that adapts would first produce a set of operations that's trying to detect some bounding boxes for the various objects in the image. Now, if this trace of operations is not enough to make a prediction, maybe it can produce some more operations that's trying to contrast some of the edges and keep producing this trace going longer and longer until it's confident enough to make a final prediction. Now I call this paradigm, where the model is adapting to the hardness of the test instance by performing variable operations as test time adaptation. So how do we think about test time adaptation formally? So the key property that we're looking for here is that as the model performs more operations on the test input, the performance on that input improves.
